name: Matrices and Determinants — Grids of Numbers
whatIsIt: A MATRIX is a rectangular grid of numbers arranged in rows and columns. Its ORDER is rows × columns (a 2×3 matrix has 2 rows, 3 columns, and 6 entries). Matrices of the same order add entry-by-entry. A DETERMINANT is a single number computed from a square matrix; for a 2×2 matrix [[a, b], [c, d]] it is ad − bc.
whyNeeded: Matrices store and transform data compactly — solving systems of equations, computer graphics rotations, networks, and machine learning all run on matrices. The determinant tells you whether a system has a unique solution and measures how a transformation scales area.
realLifeUses: Solving several equations at once (linear systems)
Rotating and scaling images in computer graphics
Storing tables of data for machine learning

vocabulary.meaning: A rectangular grid of numbers in rows and columns.
Rows × columns; e.g. a 2×3 matrix has 2 rows and 3 columns.
A single number in the matrix; there are (rows × columns) of them.
For [[a, b], [c, d]] it is ad − bc — a single number.

story.title: The Seating Grid
story.text: Miss writes the class's marks as a grid: 2 rows (two tests) and 3 columns (three students). 'That's a 2×3 matrix,' she says, 'with 2 × 3 = 6 numbers.' Then she writes a square 2×2 grid [[1, 2], [3, 4]] and asks its determinant. Priya computes ad − bc = 1×4 − 2×3 = 4 − 6 = −2. 'One number that captures the whole square,' Miss smiles.
story.extractedProblem: Find the determinant of the 2×2 matrix [[1, 2], [3, 4]].

visual.caption: A matrix is a grid: rows × columns gives its order and the number of entries (here 2 × 3 = 6).

teachingGallery.title: Order, then determinant
teachingGallery.note: Order = rows × columns. A 2×2 determinant is the diagonal product minus the anti-diagonal product.

examples.caption: For [[a, b], [c, d]], determinant = ad − bc.
ad = 4, bc = 6, so the determinant is 4 − 6 = −2.

standardMethod.summary: Order = rows × columns (and that many elements). Add matrices of equal order entry-by-entry. For a 2×2 determinant, compute ad − bc.
steps: State the order: count rows, then columns (rows × columns).
The number of elements equals rows × columns.
To add two matrices of the SAME order, add matching entries.
For a 2×2 matrix [[a, b], [c, d]], determinant = ad − bc (main diagonal minus other diagonal).
Check: a determinant is a single number, not a grid.

workedExamples.problem: How many elements are in a 2×3 matrix?

commonMistakes.mistake: Computing a determinant as ad + bc instead of ad − bc.
commonMistakes.fix: It is a SUBTRACTION: main diagonal ad MINUS the other diagonal bc.

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing the order as columns × rows.
commonMistakes.fix: Order is always ROWS × COLUMNS. A grid with 2 rows and 3 columns is 2×3, not 3×2.

easy.q: How many elements are in a 2×3 matrix?
How many elements are in a 3×3 matrix?

challenge.q: For [[3, 1], [2, 4]] a student writes 3×4 + 1×2. What's the fix?

questions.q: The order of a matrix is written as…

teachBackPrompt: Draw a 2×3 grid of numbers for a grown-up and explain its order is rows × columns = 6 elements. Then compute the determinant of [[1, 2], [3, 4]] as ad − bc = 4 − 6 = −2.

revisionCard.summary: Matrix = grid of numbers; order = ROWS × COLUMNS (= number of elements). Add matrices of equal order entry-by-entry. 2×2 determinant of [[a, b], [c, d]] = ad − bc (main diagonal minus the other). A determinant is one number, not a grid.
